questions.question: How does the phase-flip code encode the logical |0⟩ state?
questions.explanation: The phase-flip code encodes |0⟩_L = |+++⟩ and |1⟩_L = |---⟩. This is the Hadamard-transformed version of the bit-flip code's |000⟩ and |111⟩ encoding.

questions.question: What are the stabilizer generators for the phase-flip code?
questions.explanation: The phase-flip code uses X₁X₂ and X₂X₃ as stabilizers. These operators compare qubits in the X basis and can detect Z errors, which flip |+⟩ ↔ |-⟩.

questions.question: What is the relationship between the bit-flip and phase-flip codes?
questions.explanation: The phase-flip code is the Hadamard-conjugate of the bit-flip code. Applying H to each qubit transforms |000⟩↔|+++⟩, |111⟩↔|---⟩, and swaps the roles of X and Z errors.

questions.question: Why can't the phase-flip code detect X errors?

options.text: |+⟩ and |-⟩ are X eigenstates, so X doesn't change their parity
The code uses the wrong stabilizers
X errors only affect the global phase

questions.explanation: Since |+⟩ and |-⟩ are eigenstates of X (with eigenvalues +1 and -1), an X error doesn't change the X-basis parity between qubits. The X₁X₂ stabilizer sees X|+⟩=|+⟩ and X|-⟩=-|-⟩, giving the same syndrome.

questions.question: What happens when a Z error occurs on qubit 1 in an encoded |+⟩_L state?

options.text: The syndrome (1,0) is produced, identifying the error

questions.explanation: A Z₁ error changes the parity between qubits 1 and 2 (detected by X₁X₂ giving syndrome bit 1) but not between qubits 2 and 3 (X₂X₃ gives 0). The syndrome (1,0) correctly identifies qubit 1 as the error location.

questions.question: How does Shor's 9-qubit code combine the two 3-qubit codes?

options.text: Runs them in parallel on separate qubits
Uses phase-flip code for outer layer, bit-flip for inner layer
Uses 3 bit-flip codes without phase protection

questions.explanation: The Shor code first encodes with the phase-flip code (|0⟩→|+++⟩, |1⟩→|---⟩), then encodes each of those 3 qubits with the bit-flip code (|+⟩→(|000⟩+|111⟩)/√2). This gives 9 total qubits with both X and Z protection.

questions.question: The encoding circuits for bit-flip and phase-flip codes are identical except for Hadamard gates at the end.
questions.explanation: True. Both codes use the same CNOT structure for entanglement. The phase-flip code simply adds H gates to each qubit at the end, transforming |000⟩+|111⟩ into |+++⟩+|---⟩.
